title: CEO Interview with Mark Ren of Agentrys - Semiwiki
gist: Agentrys, led by former NVIDIA/IBM Research veteran Mark Ren, is building 'Agentic Design Automation' (ADA) — customer-owned, on-prem AI agent workforces that learn from a chip design team's own engineering data and workflows — with strong published results on NVIDIA's CVDP verification benchmark and a thesis that lasting competitive advantage in AI-for-chip-design comes from proprietary methodology/data rather than off-the-shelf frontier AI.

insights.claim: Agentrys is pioneering 'Agentic Design Automation' (ADA), positioned as the next transformation after EDA: automating and continuously improving the engineering workflows that determine chip product quality, engineering productivity, and time-to-market, customized to each design team's own methodology.
insights.speaker: Mark Ren
insights.so_what: Identifies a distinct emerging category (ADA) beyond traditional EDA and beyond generic coding agents, relevant for investors mapping the AI-for-chip-design competitive landscape and potential new entrants challenging incumbent EDA vendors.
insights.quote: We are pioneering Agentic Design Automation (ADA), a new paradigm for semiconductor engineering... We believe the next transformation is to automate and continuously improve the engineering workflows that determine product quality, engineering productivity, and time-to-market.

insights.claim: Agentrys' central competitive thesis is that if every competitor adopts similar frontier AI, lasting advantage cannot come from the AI model itself but must come from something a team owns — its own methodology and proprietary engineering data, learned behind the customer's firewall.
insights.speaker: Mark Ren
insights.so_what: A contrarian/against-consensus framework for AI moats in engineering: commoditized frontier models raise the industry floor but create no differentiation, so durable advantage shifts to proprietary data and workflow ownership — a lens applicable well beyond chip design.
insights.quote: if every competitor adopts similar frontier AI, where does lasting advantage come from? If AI is just another productivity tool everyone buys off the shelf, it raises the floor for the whole industry but gives no one an edge. Our answer is that the advantage must come from something a team owns
insights.locator: What keeps your customers up at night?

insights.claim: Agentrys deploys inside the customer's own environment (on-prem or private cloud), keeping RTL, netlists, PDKs, verification collateral, and engineering knowledge under the customer's control, differentiating it from vendor-controlled or cloud-only AI agent offerings.
insights.speaker: Mark Ren
insights.so_what: On-prem/air-gapped deployment addresses semiconductor customers' core concern about protecting proprietary IP, a key differentiator versus cloud-based AI coding agent competitors when selling into security-sensitive chip design organizations.
insights.quote: Customers build an agentic engineering workforce that evolves around their own workflows, methodology, engineering knowledge, and design data. We deploy inside the customer’s environment, either on-prem or in a private cloud.

insights.claim: Agentrys deliberately orchestrates across a heterogeneous, cross-vendor environment spanning commercial EDA tools and agents from multiple vendors rather than assuming a single tool ecosystem.
insights.speaker: Mark Ren
insights.so_what: A cross-vendor orchestration strategy positions Agentrys as complementary to, rather than competing head-on with, incumbent EDA giants (Synopsys, Cadence, Siemens EDA), reducing channel-conflict risk.
insights.quote: Production engineering workflows span commercial EDA tools and agents from multiple vendors as well as internal tools and infrastructure. We orchestrate across that heterogeneous environment instead of assuming a single tool ecosystem.

insights.claim: On the verification tasks of NVIDIA's public CVDP (Comprehensive Verilog Design Problems) benchmark, Agentrys' system reaches roughly 95% on both checker generation and assertion generation, and 90% on testbench stimulus generation.
insights.speaker: Mark Ren
insights.so_what: Concrete, published benchmark scores (95%/95%/90%) give investors a measurable proof point for Agentrys' claimed functional-verification capability, its strongest and most proven application area today.
insights.quote: On the verification tasks of NVIDIA’s public CVDP (Comprehensive Verilog Design Problems) benchmark, our system reaches roughly 95% on both checker generation and assertion generation, and 90% on testbench stimulus generation.
insights.locator: What application areas are your strongest?

insights.claim: Agentrys' strongest and most proven area today is functional verification (testbench stimulus generation, checker generation, assertion creation), and it is now extending into physical implementation and optimization through active customer engagements, with RTL design, analog design, and system design on its roadmap.
insights.speaker: Mark Ren
insights.so_what: Shows a phased expansion strategy from verification into physical design and beyond, giving investors a way to track Agentrys' product maturity and expanding addressable workflow surface over time.
insights.quote: Our platform is designed to span the semiconductor development lifecycle, but we are deliberately focused. Our strongest and most proven area today is functional verification... Next, we are extending into physical implementation and optimization, where we are already in active customer engagements.
insights.locator: What application areas are your strongest?

insights.claim: Agentrys includes researchers and engineers from NVIDIA, Meta, AMD, Samsung, Google, and Siemens, and its founder Mark Ren has 26 years of EDA/AI R&D experience at IBM Research and NVIDIA Research, including leading the first industrial LLM-for-chip-design effort, ChipNeMo.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: A team pedigree drawn from top chip-design and AI organizations, plus the founder's direct authorship of ChipNeMo (an early industrial LLM-for-chip-design effort), lends credibility to Agentrys' technical claims for investors assessing execution risk.
insights.quote: Mark has spearheaded the modern GPU-accelerated EDA movement, driving adoption of deep-learning frameworks to unlock performance and scalability for EDA workloads. He also led the first industrial LLM-for-chip-design effort, ChipNeMo.
insights.locator: Mark Ren has 26 years of EDA and AI R&D experience

insights.claim: Even though EDA tools have automated many parts of chip design workflows, significant engineering effort is still required to achieve high-quality results — for example, debug dominates verification time, floor planning gates physical design, and layout remains difficult for analog circuits.
insights.speaker: Mark Ren
insights.so_what: Pinpoints specific unautomated bottlenecks (debug, floor planning, analog layout) in current chip design flows — a roadmap of where AI-driven productivity gains and vendor opportunity remain largest.
insights.quote: Even though EDA tools have automated many parts of these workflows, significant engineering effort is still required to achieve high-quality results. For example, debug dominates verification time, floor planning gates physical design, and layout remains difficult for analog circuits.

insights.claim: Mark Ren observes that frontier-model providers, commercial AI agents, EDA vendors, and startups are all rapidly bringing AI into semiconductor engineering, which he interprets as validation that AI will become a standard component of chip design, with major EDA companies layering AI atop existing tools and various startups focusing on foundation models or domain-specific agents.
insights.speaker: Mark Ren
insights.so_what: Maps the competitive field for AI-in-chip-design across four categories (frontier labs, commercial agents, EDA incumbents, startups), useful for investors assessing where incumbents like Synopsys/Cadence face disruption risk versus where they are absorbing AI themselves.
insights.quote: The industry is moving rapidly. Frontier-model providers, commercial AI agents, EDA vendors, and startups are all bringing AI into semiconductor engineering. We see that as validation that AI will become a standard component of chip design.
insights.locator: What does the competitive landscape look like

insights.claim: Agentrys' engagement model follows three steps — Onboard, Evolve, Scale — starting with one or two high-value customer workflows with measurable targets, then expanding as agents adapt and build trust, moving from tier-1 semiconductor companies to AI-silicon startups.
insights.speaker: Mark Ren
insights.so_what: A land-and-expand go-to-market model (Onboard, Evolve, Scale) gives investors a framework to evaluate Agentrys' revenue-expansion potential within existing accounts, a typical enterprise SaaS growth pattern applied to chip-design AI.
insights.quote: Engagements usually follow three steps — we call it Onboard, Evolve, Scale... That arc is also our business model. Onboard to land, evolve to earn trust, scale across the organization.
